EAST
GERMANY
Deutsche Demokratische Republik · DDR
Est. 1949 · Command Economy · Soviet Bloc
WEST
GERMANY
Bundesrepublik Deutschland · BRD
Est. 1949 · Social Market Economy · Western Bloc
Economics Case Study Series · No. 1

THE TRABANT PROBLEM

East and West Germany · 1949 to 1989 · Two Systems, One Nation
Learning Targets: The 3 Key Questions Command vs. Market Economy Economic Goals and Trade-offs Price Makers vs. Price Takers Factors of Production
Fig. 1 — Germany: Map of the Occupation Areas, 1945. The four Allied powers divided Germany into zones after World War II. The Soviet zone (east) would become the GDR. The American, British and French zones (west) would become the FRG.
Fig. 2 — Children walk alongside the Berlin Wall, c. 1962. Built overnight in August 1961, the Wall stretched 96 miles through the city. Over 140 people were killed trying to cross it.
Fig. 3 — East Germans queue outside a state store, 1970s. In a command economy, prices do not signal producers to make more. Shortages and long lines were a permanent feature of daily life in the GDR.
Fig. 4 — A street full of Trabants in East Germany. Every car on the road was the same model, unchanged for decades. No competition. No alternatives. No incentive to improve.
Fig. 5 — Crowds atop the Berlin Wall at the Brandenburg Gate, November 9, 1989. Within hours of the GDR announcing it would open the border, thousands gathered to tear it down. The experiment was over.
"What if you could run the same country as two completely different economic experiments, at the same time?"

In 1949, one nation split into two. Same people, same language, same bombed-out starting line. The only difference: one half chose free markets, and the other chose to let the government plan everything. Forty years later, you could measure the results. One side had produced one of the great economic miracles of the 20th century. The other had produced... the Trabant.

Section 01 · Background
Part 1 — How Did This Happen?
Germany, Divided

World War II ended in 1945 with Germany in ruins. The four Allied powers divided it into occupation zones. The plan was temporary. It wasn't.

By 1949, two separate nations had formed. The western zones became the Federal Republic of Germany, a democracy with free markets backed by the U.S. The Soviet zone became the German Democratic Republic, a communist state controlled by a single party, despite the word "democratic" in the name.

Families were split across the new border. Berlin itself was cut in two. By 1961, more than 3.5 million East Germans had fled west, many of them doctors, engineers and teachers. The East German government's solution: build a wall overnight to stop people from leaving. It worked. It stood for 28 years. When it fell in November 1989, crowds tore it down with hammers within hours.

Part 2 — Why Economists Love This
The World's Best Economic Experiment

Comparing economic systems is usually messy. Different countries have different cultures, resources, languages and histories, so it is hard to isolate what the economic system itself actually does.

Germany is different. In 1949, the starting conditions were as close to identical as history ever produces. Same people, same language, same destroyed infrastructure, same culture. The only variable was the economic system, and it was changed completely and on purpose.

Run that experiment for 40 years, then open the border and see what happens. By 1989, West Germans were roughly three times wealthier on average. When East Germans could finally cross freely, they flooded west and abandoned nearly everything they had owned in the East, including their cars. The experiment had a clear result.

"Prosperity for all."
Ludwig Erhard, West Germany’s first Economics Minister, 1957
Section 02 · The Three Key Questions
How Each System Answered the Big Questions
East Germany · Command Economy
The Government Decides Everything

In East Germany, a central planning agency answered all three key questions for the entire economy, for every factory, farm and household.

▶ What to produce?

Government planners set production targets for every factory: how many apartments, how many shoes, how many cars. The goal was to meet everyone's basic needs equally.

▶ How to produce it?

The state owned all factories, land and major equipment, the factors of production. Workers were assigned jobs. Factory managers chased quotas, not profits.

▶ Who gets it?

The state distributed goods based on need. Housing, healthcare and education were provided to everyone. Wages were kept roughly equal across professions.

"We will overtake West Germany within ten years."
Walter Ulbricht, East German Leader, 1958
West Germany · Social Market Economy
Markets Decide, Government Protects

West Germany adopted free markets with a government safety net, what its designers called the Soziale Marktwirtschaft, or social market economy.

▶ What to produce?

Businesses and entrepreneurs decided based on what customers wanted and where profits could be made. If people would buy it, someone would make it.

▶ How to produce it?

Private companies owned the factories and equipment. Competition forced them to find efficient methods and improve constantly. Volkswagen, BMW and Mercedes competed furiously for every customer.

▶ Who gets it?

Consumers buy what they can afford. The government provides unemployment insurance, healthcare and pensions as a safety net, but does not control what most people buy or sell.

"The market delivers what planners can only promise."
Guiding principle of the Wirtschaftswunder era
Concept East Germany (Command) West Germany (Market)
Who answers the 3 Key Questions? Central government planners set all production targets Businesses and consumers, through supply and demand
Who owns the Factors of Production? The state owns land, capital and factories Private individuals and companies
Price Takers or Price Makers? The state sets all prices. Firms follow quotas, not prices Competitive firms are price takers. Monopolies can become price makers
Incentive to innovate? Very low. No competition, no profit motive High. Survival depends on beating rivals
What happens to bad products? They keep getting made. See: the Trabant, 1957 to 1991 Dropped or improved. Consumers simply stop buying them
Job security Guaranteed. The state employs everyone Not guaranteed. Depends on business performance
Consumer choice Very limited. One car, assigned housing, scarce goods Extensive. Competition produces wide variety
Section 03 · The Trabant Problem
One Car Tells the Whole Story

Nothing illustrates the gap between the two systems more vividly than their cars. East Germany produced one model, the Trabant. West Germany let its auto industry compete freely. Tap the cards to dig into what that actually meant.

THE TRABANT  ·  Produced 1957 to 1991
Two-stroke engine  ·  Plastic body
flip for facts
WHY THE TRABANT WAS TERRIBLE
⚡
Its two-stroke engine produced about 25 horsepower. A modern riding lawnmower produces more power. Top speed was around 70 mph, downhill, with a tailwind.
🧶
The body was made from Duroplast, a mix of compressed wool fibers and resin. It couldn't be recycled. When the factories closed, East Germany had no idea what to do with the millions of abandoned Trabants.
💨
Trabants ran on a fuel and oil mixture that produced a thick, foul-smelling cloud of blue smoke. Pedestrians could smell one coming from two blocks away.
🏆
The Trabant has been voted the worst car ever made in multiple polls. It was still in production in 1991 using technology designed in 1957. No competition will do that to a product.
click to flip back ↻
1
Car model available
in all of East Germany
tap to dig in
The Trabant was the only consumer car produced in East Germany. State planners decided one model was enough. No competition meant no pressure to offer anything better, different or cheaper. This is the logical outcome when the government, not the market, answers "what to produce?"
11
Years average wait
to buy a Trabant
tap to dig in
You placed your order, paid upfront, and waited... on average 11 years. In a command economy, prices don't signal factories to make more when demand rises. Supply and demand are permanently disconnected. The result is a permanent shortage, with no mechanism to fix it.
30
Years without a
meaningful redesign
tap to dig in
The Trabant still used a two-stroke engine and a body made from compressed wool and resin when production ended in 1991, technology from the 1950s. Factory managers were rewarded for hitting their production quota, not for improving the product. Without competition, there was simply no reason to change.
~0
Trabants kept after
the Wall fell
tap to dig in
When East Germans could finally access Western cars, they abandoned Trabants in parking lots by the thousands. Worthless on any open market. This is people "voting with their feet," and it tells us something that no official statistic can: given a real choice, people revealed exactly which system they preferred.
Section 04 · Economic Goals
It's More Complicated Than You Might Think

Here is where it gets interesting and honest. East Germany was not a failure at everything. West Germany was not perfect. When you score each system against the standard economic goals, the picture is more nuanced than "capitalism good, communism bad."

Economic Goal
East Germany
West Germany
Economic Growth
✗ Weak
✓ Strong
Efficiency and Innovation
✗ Weak see: the Trabant
✓ Strong VW, BMW, Mercedes...
Economic Freedom
✗ Very Low
✓ High
Stable Prices
~ Mixed prices fixed, but shortages
✓ Moderate
Full Employment
✓ Strong state guaranteed all jobs
~ Moderate
Equity and Equal Distribution
✓ High wages kept roughly equal
~ Mixed
Social Welfare and Security
✓ Strong free housing, healthcare
~ Strong safety net
Environmental Sustainability
✗ Very Weak massive industrial pollution
~ Mixed
★ Discussion
East Germany Failed. But It's Worth Asking: At What?

East Germany did not fail at everything. It failed at the goals it was not trying to achieve: growth, efficiency, innovation and freedom. On the goals it was pursuing, equality of wages, guaranteed employment and universal social services, it actually performed. The problem is that it sacrificed so much to get there, and ultimately could not sustain even those wins without a functioning economy underneath them.

West Germany's story is just as instructive, and it is often misread. West Germany was not pure free market capitalism. It was a mixed economy, one that used markets to drive growth and innovation while the government kept a strong safety net in place. The result was a system that performed well across nearly every goal simultaneously: strong growth, high efficiency, real freedom and still solid welfare, employment and social security. That balance is the point. The lesson is not "markets beat planning." It is that well-designed mixed economies can pursue multiple goals at once, while ideologically pure systems tend to win on some goals and collapse on others.

If you had to live in one of these countries in 1975, not knowing anything about their future, which would you choose and why? Does your answer change if you are a factory worker? A doctor? A single parent? What does that tell you about who economic goals actually serve?

Check for Understanding
Apply the Framework. Don't Just Describe It.
  • In East Germany, who answered the three key questions and what did they use to make those decisions? In West Germany, who answered them and what drove their choices?
  • The Trabant went virtually unchanged for 30 years. Use the concepts of price makers, price takers and competition to explain why. What would have needed to change for East Germany to build a better car?
  • East Germany had essentially zero unemployment. Is that automatically a good thing? What does it tell us about the trade-offs between economic goals?
  • Synthesis: When the Wall fell, East Germans abandoned their Trabants and flooded west. In economic terms, what were they revealing about which goals they personally prioritized? Does a population "voting with their feet" tell us something markets and ballots cannot?
Key Terms
Vocabulary for This Case Study
Command Economy
A system where the government answers the three key questions, deciding what gets produced, how, and who receives it. Also called a planned economy.
Free Market Economy
A system where individuals and businesses make decisions through voluntary exchange, guided by supply and demand rather than government direction.
Mixed Economy
A system combining free market and government elements. Both Germanys were technically mixed. No pure version of either system exists in practice.
Factors of Production
Land, Labor, Capital and Entrepreneurship, the inputs needed to produce goods and services. A core question of any economic system: who owns and controls these?
Price Taker
A firm in a competitive market that must accept the going market price. It has no power to set its own. Forced to compete on quality and efficiency to survive.
Price Maker
A firm with enough market power to set its own price, without losing customers. In East Germany, the state was the ultimate price maker. In the West, monopolies can sometimes fill this role.
Profit Motive
The incentive to earn profit drives firms in market economies to innovate and respond to consumers. Its absence in command economies is a key reason for stagnation and, yes, the Trabant problem.
Economic Trade-offs
Gains in one economic goal often come at the cost of another. East Germany gained equity but sacrificed efficiency. West Germany gained growth but accepted inequality. There is no free lunch.
